model_findings.description: `--host` accepts arbitrary bind addresses and `uvicorn.run` uses them directly, so `ab serve --openai --host 0.0.0.0` exposes an unauthenticated HTTP endpoint that can invoke local agent CLIs using the user's credentials and repository context. For local-only tooling, non-loopback binds should require an explicit remote-enable flag or be rejected.

`/v1/chat/completions` accepts unbounded message counts and content sizes, then flattens the full payload and synchronously starts a backend subprocess per request with no body-size, prompt-size, or concurrency limits. A local or accidentally exposed client can consume memory, process slots, backend quota, and up to the full backend timeout per request.

The request model allows unknown OpenAI fields but `chat_completions` ignores `stream`; clients sending `stream: true` will receive a normal JSON completion with HTTP 200 instead of an SSE stream or an explicit unsupported-feature error. This breaks OpenAI-compatible clients that switch parsing behavior based on the requested streaming mode.

Gemini, Claude, and Hermes prompts are passed as command-line arguments rather than stdin or a temporary file. Request contents may include secrets and can be visible to local process-list observers while the subprocess runs; large prompts also risk OS argument-length failures before the backend receives the request.

The new activity_schema gate silently continues when an error-correction activity has a missing or non-list `items` field, and also skips non-mapping entries inside the list. That means malformed writer output such as `items: {incorrect: ...}` or a top-level alias shape can pass this gate with `checked: 0`, then fail later in `ActivityParser._parse_error_correction` with an unhelpful runtime error or publish an empty activity. Since this gate is intended to enforce the authoring schema early, strict-schema activity types should report these shapes as violations instead of skipping them.
